subject: Statistics
title: HARKing: Hypothesizing After Results
description: A tiny lesson with a big payoff: HARKing: Hypothesizing After Results.

hook.text: A study finds that left-handed people earn more. The researcher writes: 'We predicted left-handers earn more.'

buildup.text: HARKing means forming your hypothesis after seeing the results, then pretending you predicted it all along.

discovery.text: Researchers test many hypotheses, then present the significant one as if it was planned all along.

twist.text: HARKing is invisible to readers. Published papers rarely admit which hypotheses came after the data.

climax.text: Registered reports require hypotheses before data collection. This makes HARKing structurally impossible.

punchline.text: Predictions made after the fact aren't predictions.

quiz.question: What is HARKing?
options: Hypothesizing after results are known — pretending you predicted what the data showed
